content:
# Numbers Chapter 14 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 164-166 | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/NUM_014_page_001.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pa_0122-0123

---

**1** And the entire community lifted up their voices and cried out, and the people wept through that night.

**2** And all the Sons of Israel grumbled against Moses and against Aaron. And the whole assembly said to them, "If only we had died in the land of Egypt! Or if only we had died here in this wilderness!

**3** Why is YHWH bringing us into this land only to fall by the sword? Our wives and children will be taken as plunder! Would it not be better for us to go back to Egypt?"

**4** And they said to one another, "Let us appoint a leader and go back to Egypt."

**5** And Moses and Aaron fell on their faces before the entire assembled community of the Sons of Israel.

**6** And Joshua son of Nun and Caleb son of Jephunneh, who were among those who had scouted the land, tore their clothes

**7** and said to the whole community of the Sons of Israel, "The land we passed through to scout is surpassingly, surpassingly good!

**8** If YHWH delights in us, he will bring us into this land and give it to us -- a land flowing with milk and honey.

**9** Only do not rebel against YHWH! And do not fear the people of the land -- they are bread for us! Their shade of protection has departed from over them, and YHWH is with us. Do not fear them!"

**10** And the whole assembly spoke of stoning them with stones. But the glory of YHWH appeared at the Tent of Meeting to all the Sons of Israel.

**11** And YHWH said to Moses, "How long will this people despise me? And how long will they refuse to trust me, despite all the signs I have performed in their midst?

**12** I will strike them with a plague and dispossess them, and I will make you into a nation greater and mightier than they are."

**13** And Moses said to YHWH, "But the Egyptians will hear of it! For you brought this people up from among them by your power,

**14** and they will tell it to the inhabitants of this land. They have heard that you, YHWH, are in the midst of this people -- that you, YHWH, are seen eye to eye, that your cloud stands over them, and that you go before them in a pillar of cloud by day and in a pillar of fire by night.

**15** And if you put this entire people to death as one man, then the nations who have heard your fame will say,

**16** 'Because YHWH was not able to bring this people into the land he swore to give them, he slaughtered them in the wilderness.'

**17** And now, please, let the power of my Lord be great, just as you declared when you said,

**18** 'YHWH is slow to anger and abundant in faithful love, bearing wrongdoing and rebellion -- yet he will certainly not leave the guilty unpunished, visiting the consequences of the parents' wrongdoing on their children to the third and fourth generation.'

**19** Please forgive the wrongdoing of this people, according to the greatness of your faithful love, just as you have carried this people from Egypt until now."

**20** And YHWH said, "I have forgiven, according to your word.

**21** But as surely as I live, and as all the earth will be filled with the glory of YHWH --

**22** surely none of the men who have seen my glory and my signs that I performed in Egypt and in the wilderness, yet have tested me now these ten times and have not listened to my voice --

**23** they will never see the land I swore to their fathers. And none who despised me will see it.

**24** But my servant Caleb -- because a different spirit was with him and he has followed me completely -- I will bring him into the land where he went, and his descendants will possess it.

**25** Now the Amalekites and the Canaanites dwell in the valley. Tomorrow, turn around and set out into the wilderness by way of the Sea of Reeds."

**26** And YHWH spoke to Moses and to Aaron, saying,

**27** "How long shall I bear with this wicked community that grumbles against me? I have heard the complaints that the Sons of Israel are raising against me.

**28** Say to them: 'As surely as I live' -- declares YHWH -- 'just as you have spoken in my hearing, so I will do to you.

**29** In this wilderness your corpses will fall -- all of you who were counted in the census, all who are numbered from twenty years old and upward, who have grumbled against me.

**30** You will certainly not come into the land where I raised my hand to settle you -- except Caleb son of Jephunneh and Joshua son of Nun.

**31** And your children, whom you said would become plunder -- I will bring them in, and they will know the land that you rejected.

**32** But as for you, your corpses will fall in this wilderness.

**33** And your children will be wanderers in the wilderness for forty years, bearing the burden of your unfaithfulness, until the last of your corpses lies in the wilderness.

**34** According to the number of days you scouted the land -- forty days, a year for each day -- you will bear your wrongdoing for forty years, and you will know what it means to have me against you.'

**35** I, YHWH, have spoken. I will surely do this to this entire wicked community that has banded together against me. In this wilderness they will be spent. And there they will die."

**36** And the men whom Moses had sent to scout the land -- who returned and made the whole community grumble against him by spreading a ruinous report about the land --

**37** those men who spread the evil report about the land died by plague before YHWH.

**38** But Joshua son of Nun and Caleb son of Jephunneh remained alive, out of all those men who had gone to scout the land.

**39** And Moses spoke these words to all the Sons of Israel, and the people mourned deeply.

**40** And they rose early in the morning and went up to the heights of the hill country, saying, "Here we are! We will go up to the place YHWH spoke of, for we have sinned."

**41** And Moses said, "Why now are you transgressing the command of YHWH? It will not succeed.

**42** Do not go up, for YHWH is not in your midst -- or you will be struck down before your enemies.

**43** For the Amalekites and the Canaanites are there before you, and you will fall by the sword. Because you have turned back from following YHWH, YHWH will not be with you."

**44** Yet in their presumption they surged up toward the heights of the hill country, even though neither the Ark of the Covenant of YHWH nor Moses moved from the camp.

**45** And the Amalekites and the Canaanites who lived in that hill country came down and struck them and crushed them, all the way to Hormah.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 1 -- "the people wept that night" (וַיִּבְכּוּ הָעָם בַּלַּיְלָה הַהוּא / vayyivku ha'am ballaylah hahu):** The verb בָּכָה (*bakhah*) describes loud, audible weeping -- not silent tears but communal wailing. The definite article on "that night" (הַהוּא) marks it as a night of infamy: this is *the* night, the one that will define a generation. Rabbinic tradition (b. Taanit 29a) connects this night to the ninth of Av, the date of both Temple destructions -- "they wept for nothing, so I will establish for them a weeping for generations." Whether historically grounded or not, the tradition captures the narrative logic: weeping born of faithlessness becomes the seed of genuine catastrophe. The LXX Vaticanus opens with καὶ ἀναλαβοῦσα πᾶσα ἡ συναγωγὴ ἔδωκαν φωνὴν αὐτῶν ("and the whole congregation, having lifted up, gave their voices"), emphasizing the collective, deliberate nature of the outcry.

**Verse 3 -- "our wives and children will be taken as plunder" (נָשֵׁינוּ וְטַפֵּנוּ יִהְיוּ לָבַז / nashenu vetappenu yihyu lavaz):** The word טַף (*tap*) refers to small children, toddlers -- the most vulnerable members of the community. The cruel irony of this verse unfolds in verse 31, where YHWH declares that it is precisely these children whom he will bring safely into the land, while the adults who claimed to be protecting them will die in the wilderness. The people's stated reason for refusing the land -- concern for their families -- is exposed as a pretext. They are not protecting their children; they are projecting their own terror onto them.

**Verse 4 -- "let us choose a leader and go back to Egypt" (נִתְּנָה רֹאשׁ וְנָשׁוּבָה מִצְרָיְמָה / nittenah rosh venashuvah mitzraymah):** The phrase נִתְּנָה רֹאשׁ ("let us appoint a head") is a direct repudiation of both Moses' leadership and YHWH's sovereignty. The proposal to return to Egypt is not merely geographical retreat but theological apostasy: it reverses the entire exodus narrative, undoing the foundational act of YHWH's redemption. The verb שׁוּב (*shuv*, "to return/turn back") is loaded in prophetic literature, where it usually means repentance -- turning back *to* YHWH. Here it means turning back *from* YHWH, the precise inversion of what the word should mean for Israel. The LXX uses δῶμεν ἀρχηγόν ("let us give ourselves a ruler"), heightening the political rebellion.

**Verse 9 -- "they will be food for us" (כִּי לַחְמֵנוּ הֵם / ki lachmenu hem):** Literally "they are our bread." The metaphor is startling in its confidence -- Joshua and Caleb reduce the terrifying Canaanites from giant warriors to a meal to be consumed. The word לֶחֶם (*lechem*, "bread/food") is the most basic staple of life, implying that conquering these peoples will be as natural and necessary as eating. The phrase סָר צִלָּם מֵעֲלֵיהֶם (*sar tzillam me'alehem*, "their shade/protection has turned aside from upon them") is equally vivid: צֵל (*tzel*) means shadow, shade, shelter -- the protective covering that shields from the desert sun. Their divine protection has departed; they are exposed. The contrast is stark: Israel has YHWH's shade; the Canaanites have lost theirs.

**Verse 11 -- "how long will they refuse to trust me" (עַד־אָנָה לֹא־יַאֲמִינוּ בִי / ad-anah lo ya'aminu vi):** The verb אָמַן (*aman*) is the root behind "amen" and "emunah" (faith/faithfulness). YHWH is not asking how long they will disobey a rule but how long they will refuse to find him trustworthy -- how long they will look at the evidence of his character and conclude he cannot be relied upon. The word בִּי (*vi*, "in me") makes it personal: the offense is not abstract theological doubt but relational betrayal. After ten demonstrations of his power and faithfulness (the plagues, the sea crossing, manna, quail, water from rock), they still do not regard him as dependable. The number "ten" (עֶשֶׂר) in this context may be literal or may function as the Hebrew expression for "repeatedly, more than enough times."

**Verse 12 -- "I will make you into a nation greater and mightier" (וְאֶעֱשֶׂה אֹתְךָ לְגוֹי־גָּדוֹל וְעָצוּם / ve'e'eseh otkha legoy-gadol ve'atzum):** This offer echoes the original promise to Abraham (Genesis 12:2) but proposes to restart it through Moses alone, discarding the current generation entirely. The theological gravity is immense: YHWH is offering to dissolve the covenant community and begin anew. Moses' refusal of this offer in the following verses is one of the great moments of intercessory leadership in scripture -- he declines personal greatness for the sake of the people and, more profoundly, for the sake of YHWH's own reputation among the nations.

**Verses 13-16 -- Moses' intercession:** Moses' argument is remarkable because it appeals not to Israel's merit but to YHWH's reputation. His logic runs: the nations know you delivered this people; if you destroy them, the nations will conclude you were *unable* to complete the delivery (verse 16: יִכֹּלֶת, "ability/power"). Moses frames divine mercy as a matter of divine honor. The word שְׁמוּעָה (*shemu'ah*, "report/fame" -- what the nations have "heard") connects back to the scouting narrative: there was a bad report (*dibbah*) about the land; now Moses fears there will be a bad report about YHWH. The verb שָׁחַט (*shachat*, "slaughter") in verse 16 is sacrificial language -- it implies ritual killing, as though YHWH would be treating his own people like animals for slaughter. Moses uses deliberately provocative language to press his case.

**Verse 18 -- the divine character formula (נֹשֵׂא עָוֹן וָפֶשַׁע / nosei avon vafesha):** Moses quotes YHWH's own self-revelation from Exodus 34:6-7, the aftermath of the golden calf disaster. The key terms are: אֶרֶךְ אַפַּיִם (*erekh appayim*, "long of nostrils" -- slow to anger, because the nostrils flare in rage); רַב־חֶסֶד (*rav-chesed*, "abundant in *chesed*" -- the covenantal faithful love that persists despite betrayal); נֹשֵׂא עָוֹן (*nosei avon*, "carrying/bearing wrongdoing" -- YHWH lifts the burden of sin off the people and bears it himself); וְנַקֵּה לֹא יְנַקֶּה (*venakkeh lo yenakkeh*, "yet certainly not clearing" -- there is forgiveness but not erasure of consequences). Moses is holding YHWH to his own declared character. The word פֶּשַׁע (*pesha*, "rebellion/transgression") is the strongest Hebrew word for sin -- it denotes willful, deliberate defiance, not mere mistake. Moses does not minimize what the people have done; he maximizes who YHWH is.

**Verse 19 -- "according to the greatness of your faithful love" (כְּגֹדֶל חַסְדֶּךָ / kegodel chasdekha):** The word חֶסֶד (*chesed*) is the untranslatable center of Israel's theology -- it encompasses loyal love, covenant fidelity, mercy, kindness, and tenacious commitment. I render it "faithful love" to capture both the affective warmth and the covenantal obligation. Moses asks that the measure of forgiveness match the measure of *chesed* -- and since *chesed* is immeasurable, the forgiveness must be comprehensive. The verb נָשָׂא (*nasa*, "forgive") literally means "to lift, to carry" -- forgiveness is portrayed as YHWH picking up the crushing weight of Israel's sin and carrying it away.

**Verse 20 -- "I have forgiven, as you asked" (סָלַחְתִּי כִּדְבָרֶךָ /alachti kidvarekha):** The Hebrew is stunningly concise -- two words for an act of cosmic mercy. The verb סָלַח (*salach*) is used exclusively of divine forgiveness in the Hebrew Bible; humans never *salach* one another. It is a divine prerogative, not a human capacity. The phrase כִּדְבָרֶךָ ("according to your word") acknowledges Moses' intercession as the immediate cause, yet what follows (verses 21-35) reveals that forgiveness does not mean absence of consequence. The generation is pardoned from annihilation but not from accountability. This is one of the most theologically dense moments in the Torah: grace and justice coexist without contradiction.

**Verse 24 -- "a different spirit" (רוּחַ אַחֶרֶת / ruach acheret):** The word רוּחַ (*ruach*) means spirit, wind, breath -- the animating force within a person. Caleb possesses a *different* spirit, an *acheret* spirit -- one that runs counter to the fearful consensus. The phrase וַיְמַלֵּא אַחֲרָי (*vayemale' acharay*, "and he filled after me" -- i.e., "followed me wholeheartedly") uses the verb מָלֵא (*male'*, "to fill") in a unique construction: Caleb *filled up* his following of YHWH, leaving no room for doubt or reservation. Where the other scouts were half-hearted, Caleb was full -- filled to capacity with trust.

**Verse 33 -- "your children will wander as shepherds" (וּבְנֵיכֶם יִהְיוּ רֹעִים בַּמִּדְבָּר / uveneikhem yihyu ro'im bamidbar):** The word רֹעִים (*ro'im*) can mean either "shepherds" or "wanderers" -- the ambiguity is deliberate. The children will live the nomadic pastoral life, tending flocks in the wilderness, bearing the burden of their parents' faithlessness. The phrase נֹשְׂאִים אֶת־זְנוּתֵיכֶם (*nos'im et-zenuteikhem*, "bearing your whoring/unfaithfulness") uses the shocking metaphor of זְנוּת (*zenut*, sexual infidelity/prostitution) for Israel's spiritual betrayal. The wilderness generation's children carry -- physically, in their bodies and their wandering -- the consequences of their parents' refusal to trust YHWH. The forty years are not arbitrary punishment but measured justice: one year for each day of the scouting mission (verse 34), a precise correspondence between the sin's duration and the sentence's length.

**Verse 34 -- "you will know what it means to have me against you" (וִידַעְתֶּם אֶת־תְּנוּאָתִי / vida'tem et-tenu'ati):** The word תְּנוּאָה (*tenu'ah*) appears only here in the Hebrew Bible, making its precise meaning debated. It likely derives from נוּא (*nu'*, "to hinder, to oppose, to frustrate"), yielding a meaning like "my opposition" or "my frustration/withdrawal." Some render it "my displeasure" or "my estrangement." I chose "what it means to have me against you" to capture the relational devastation: the God who fought *for* Israel at the Sea will now be the one who stands *against* them. The experiential verb יָדַע (*yada'*, "to know") indicates this will not be abstract theology but lived reality -- they will *know* YHWH's opposition in their bones, in their dying.

**Verse 44 -- "yet in their presumption they pushed forward" (וַיַּעְפִּלוּ לַעֲלוֹת / vayya'pilu la'alot):** The verb עָפַל (*afal*) means to swell up, to be presumptuous, to act with reckless boldness. It conveys arrogance masquerading as courage -- the people who were too afraid to go when YHWH commanded now charge forward when YHWH forbids. The word captures a particular kind of spiritual failure: timing obedience to one's own convenience rather than to God's command. Delayed obedience and premature obedience are both disobedience. The absence of both the Ark and Moses from their advance (noted explicitly in the text) signals total divine abandonment of the enterprise. They march without the symbols of YHWH's presence, which means they march without YHWH.

**Verse 45 -- "beating them back as far as Hormah" (וַיַּכּוּם וַיַּכְּתוּם עַד־הַחָרְמָה / vayyakkum vayyakketum ad-hachormah):** The name חָרְמָה (*Chormah*) derives from חֵרֶם (*cherem*, "devoted to destruction/ban") -- the very concept of holy war that Israel was supposed to wage *against* the Canaanites. The irony is devastating: the place whose name means "destruction" becomes the site of Israel's own destruction. The double verb construction (וַיַּכּוּם וַיַּכְּתוּם, "struck them and crushed them") conveys thorough, humiliating defeat. The chapter that began with weeping ends with military disaster, completing a tragic arc: fear led to rebellion, rebellion to judgment, and presumptuous defiance to defeat. The people who refused to fight when faith demanded it were broken when presumption drove them forward without YHWH.
